charset=UTF-8]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d1f120ab-496b-4ac4-8175-c2bb63c72bd0"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">HTTP response body</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[["admin","observer1"]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><h4>Unassign a role from a particular user</h4><p>Sending HTTP DELETE to <em>/admin/roles/{roleId}/users/{username}</em> unassigns a particular role from a specified user.</p><p><br /></p><p><strong>HTTP Response</strong></p><p>204 with empty body.</p><p><br /></p><h4>Unassign a project role from a particular user</h4><p>Sending DELETE to <em>_/osmc/workspaces/\{workspaceId\}/resources/\{resourceId\}/roles/\{roleId\}/users/\{username\}_</em> unassigns a project role from a particular user.</p><p><strong>HTTP Status</strong></p><p>204 with empty body.</p><p><br /></p><h4>Assign a role to a particular user</h4><p>Sending POST to <em>/osmc/admin/roles/{roleId}/users</em> assigns a role to a user.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b73926ca-e6c3-4f0a-a22b-9675af71c27d"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">HTTP request header</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Content-Type: text/plain]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="43c88f46-412f-4ba3-b8ad-e4849cd86cf9"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">HTTP request body</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[user1,user2]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><strong>HTTP Status</strong></p><p>201 with empty body</p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303989591 space=DEVG version=1 -->
## PAGE 00032: (2026x Refresh1) Comparing projects for testing purposes

- page_id: `303989591`
- space_key: `DEVG`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG/pages/303989591/2026x+Refresh1+Comparing+projects+for+testing+purposes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T09:44:44.000+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Developer Guides > (2026x Refresh1) Developer Guides Home > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) General > (2026x Refresh1) Plugins > (2026x Refresh1) Creating test cases
- labels: ['projects-comparator', 'model-comparator', 'model-comparator-filter', 'diagram-comparator']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To compare two projects for testing purpose, the test framework provides the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.tests.common.comparators.ProjectsComparator* class with the *compare(Project[,](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/tests/common/comparators/ProjectsComparator.html#compare-com.nomagic.magicdraw.core.Project-com.nomagic.magicdraw.core.Project-)Project[)](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/tests/common/comparators/ProjectsComparator.html#compare-com.nomagic.magicdraw.core.Project-com.nomagic.magicdraw.core.Project-)* method for comparing two projects (see the following figure). The default implementation of *ProjectComparator* can be created using the *createProjectComparator(java.lang.String)* method of the *MagicDrawTestCase* class. Projects can be compared by checking their model elements and element symbols in diagrams for equality.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The model elements comparison can be configured using the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.tests.common.comparators.ModelComparator* interface. Developers may exclude some model elements from the comparison by providing the custom *com.nomagic.magicdraw.tests.common.comparators.ModelComparatorFilter* interface implementation. Please note that the default implementation of *ProjectComparator* does not compare the diagram information table, root model comment, and elements from used projects.

The default implementation of *com.nomagic.magicdraw.tests.common.comparators.DiagramComparator* compares diagrams for changes by analyzing a location and a size of the symbols presented in diagrams. The graphical differences of compared diagrams can be saved in the graphical PNG file using the *saveDiffToImageFiles(java.io.File)* method of the *DiagramComparator*interface.

Developers may create their own comparators for the custom diagrams and model elements comparison by implementing *ModelComparator* and *DiagramComparator* interfaces. The custom *Model* and *Diagram* comparators implementation can be set to *ProjectComparator* using appropriate setter methods.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><div class="WordSection1"><p>To compare two projects for testing purpose, the test framework provides the <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.tests.common.comparators.ProjectsComparator</em> class with the <em>compare(Project<a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/tests/common/comparators/ProjectsComparator.html#compare-com.nomagic.magicdraw.core.Project-com.nomagic.magicdraw.core.Project-">, </a>Project<a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/tests/common/comparators/ProjectsComparator.html#compare-com.nomagic.magicdraw.core.Project-com.nomagic.magicdraw.core.Project-">)</a></em> method for comparing two projects (see the following figure). The default implementation of <em>ProjectComparator</em> can be created using the <em>createProjectComparator(java.lang.String)</em> method of the <em>MagicDrawTestCase</em> class. Projects can be compared by checking their model elements and element symbols in diagrams for equality.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Project comparator provided by test framework" ac:alt="Project comparator provided by test framework"><ri:attachment ri:filename="project_comparator.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Comparing projects for testing purposes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>The model elements comparison can be configured using the <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.tests.common.comparators.ModelComparator</em> interface. Developers may exclude some model elements from the comparison by providing the custom <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.tests.common.comparators.ModelComparatorFilter</em> interface implementation. Please note that the default implementation of <em>ProjectComparator</em> does not compare the diagram information table, root model comment, and elements from used projects.</p><p>The default implementation of <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.tests.common.comparators.DiagramComparator</em> compares diagrams for changes by analyzing a location and a size of the symbols presented in diagrams. The graphical differences of compared diagrams can be saved in the graphical PNG file using the <em>saveDiffToImageFiles(java.io.File)</em> method of the <em>DiagramComparator </em>interface.</p><p>Developers may create their own comparators for the custom diagrams and model elements comparison by implementing <em>ModelComparator</em> and <em>DiagramComparator</em> interfaces. The custom <em>Model</em> and <em>Diagram</em> comparators implementation can be set to <em>ProjectComparator</em> using appropriate setter methods.</p></div></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303989589 space=DEVG version=1 -->
## PAGE 00033: (2026x Refresh1) Compilation classpath

- page_id: `303989589`
- space_key: `DEVG`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG/pages/303989589/2026x+Refresh1+Compilation+classpath
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T09:44:43.825+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Developer Guides > (2026x Refresh1) Developer Guides Home > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) General > (2026x Refresh1) Plugins
- labels: ['patch', 'brand', 'jar']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Core libraries**

The correct classpath must be specified when compiling your (plugin) code or running `*.jar` files from *`<modeling tool installation directory>\lib`*, as well as all its subdirectories must be included into the classpath.

You can open the properties file in`*<modeling tool installation directory>\bin*`and check the `CLASSPATH` property to be sure what files must be included.

Make sure that following *.jar files are added in such order:

1. brand.jar (if the file exists) . This file is needed if the Cameo Enterprise Architecture program is used. MagicDraw does not requires this file.
2. brand_api.jar (if the file exists)*
3. Rest *.jar files. Order of them is not important

Make sure that only the *.jar files coming with the current program version you are included. If you have upgraded your modeling tool from an earlier version, the *lib* directory may contain files from the earlier version. Do not include these obsolete *.jar files.

**Plugins libraries**

Keep in mind that some Open API classes may come from plugins (like the SysML plugin, for example). In this case, make sure that *jar*files from a plugin directory are added to the compiled classpath. For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Identifying plugin dependencies' space=''] page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>Core libraries</strong></p><p>The correct classpath must be specified when compiling your (plugin) code or running <code>*.jar</code> files from <em><code>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\lib</code></em>, as well as all its subdirectories must be included into the classpath.</p><p><span>You can open the properties file in </span><code><em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\bin</em></code><span> and check the <code>CLASSPATH</code> property to be sure what files must be included.</span></p><p>Make sure that following *.jar files are added in such order:</p><ol><li><code>brand.jar</code> (if the file exists)<span>. </span><span>This file is needed if the Cameo Enterprise Architecture program is used. </span><span>MagicDraw does not requires this file.</span></li><li>brand_api.jar (if the file exists)*</li><li>Rest <code>*.jar</code> files. Order of them is not important<br /><br /></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="379fd914-ffb0-475a-8fe2-bf0ddd4645e7"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Make sure that only the *.jar files coming with the current program version you are included. If you have upgraded your modeling tool from an earlier version, the <em>lib</em> directory may contain files from the earlier version. Do not include these obsolete *.jar files.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><strong>Plugins libraries</strong></p><p>Keep in mind that some Open API classes may come from plugins (like the SysML plugin, for example). In this case, make sure that <em>jar </em>files from a plugin directory are added to the compiled classpath. For more information, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Identifying plugin dependencies" /></ac:link> page.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303989034 space=DEVG version=1 -->
## PAGE 00034: (2026x Refresh1) Compiler API

- page_id: `303989034`
- space_key: `DEVG`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG/pages/303989034/2026x+Refresh1+Compiler+API
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T09:44:14.104+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Developer Guides > (2026x Refresh1) Developer Guides Home > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) UML-based > (2026x Refresh1) SysML v1 > (2026x Refresh1) Alf Plugin Developer Guide
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The *AlfCompiler* class provides the basic interface for compiling Alf code. The processing of a single piece of Alf text is called a *compilation,* which takes place in two steps:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Compiler API: Parsing' space=''] (including constraint checking)
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Compiler API: Mapping' space='']

The API allows you to carry out these steps individually (which is useful if, for example, you wish to parse but not map some code) or using a single call.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>The <em>AlfCompiler</em> class provides the basic interface for compiling Alf code. The processing of a single piece of Alf text is called a <em>compilation,</em> which takes place in two steps:</p><ol><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Compiler API: Parsing" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Parsing]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (including constraint checking)</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Compiler API: Mapping" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Mapping]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ol><p>The API allows you to carry out these steps individually (which is useful if, for example, you wish to parse but not map some code) or <span class="confluence-link">using a single call</span>.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c9e705e7-c70e-4d93-b5f0-d11eaf392bbb"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">
       Related Pages
      </ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="f1fa4f0e-d74d-43ad-a221-703585bb068a" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303989253 space=DEVG version=1 -->
## PAGE 00035: (2026x Refresh1) Compiler API: Mapping

- page_id: `303989253`
- space_key: `DEVG`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG/pages/303989253/2026x+Refresh1+Compiler+API+Mapping
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T09:44:27.486+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Developer Guides > (2026x Refresh1) Developer Guides Home > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) UML-based > (2026x Refresh1) SysML v1 > (2026x Refresh1) Alf Plugin Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Compiler API
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

*Mapping* is the process of generating a UML activity model from the abstract syntax representation of some Alf code. If this mapping is successful, then the Alf compiler updates the context element using the generated elements. How this update is done depends on what the context element is:

- If the context element is an Activity, the nodes and edges of the Activity are replaced with those generated by the compilation.
- If the context element is an Opaque Behavior or Opaque Action, a CompiledRepresentation stereotype is applied with its behavior tag pointing to the compiled Behavior.
- If the context element is an Opaque Expression, its behavior property is set to the compiled Behavior.

In addition, there may be some generated elements that cannot be stored in conjunction with the context element. In particular, generated Instance Specifications are stored in the nearest Package containing the context element, and generated template instantations are stored in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='The Alf compiler' space='MAA'] package.

If you have successfully parsed some Alf code text, then you can map that code by calling the *map* method. (If the parse was not successful, then calling *map* has no effect.) Normally, unless there is a system error, mapping should always complete successfully. Since mapping results in an update to the UML model, the *map* method should be called within a MagicDraw session. This can be done conveniently using the *AlfActionUtil.executeSession* method, which also ensures that any automatic Alf compilation is turned off during the session, so new compilations are not accidentally triggered by updates from the mapping process.

```java

```

Rather than separately parsing and mapping some Alf text, you can attempt to do both with one call to the *compile* method. This method first parses some text for a given context element and, if that is successful, maps it and updates the UML model appropriately with the results of the mapping. If the parse is not successful, then compilation errors are available using the *getCompilerErrors* method, as after a call to the *parse* method. As for the *map* method, the *compile* method should be called within a MagicDraw session, since it may potentially update the UML model if a mapping is carried out.

```java

```

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Compiler API: Parsing' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><em>Mapping</em> is the process of generating a UML activity model from the abstract syntax representation of some Alf code. If this mapping is successful, then the Alf compiler updates the context element using the generated elements. How this update is done depends on what the context element is:</p><ul><li>If the context element is an Activity, the nodes and edges of the Activity are replaced with those generated by the compilation.</li><li>If the context element is an Opaque Behavior or Opaque Action, a <em>CompiledRepresentation</em> stereotype is applied with its <em>behavior</em> tag pointing to the compiled Behavior.</li><li>If the context element is an Opaque Expression, its <em>behavior</em> property is set to the compiled Behavior.</li></ul><p>In addition, there may be some generated elements that cannot be stored in conjunction with the context element. In particular, generated Instance Specifications are stored in the nearest Package containing the context element, and generated template instantations are stored in the <ac:link ac:anchor="template-bindings-package"><ri:page ri:space-key="MAA" ri:content-title="The Alf compiler" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[$$Template Bindings]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> package.</p><p>If you have successfully parsed some Alf code text, then you can map that code by calling the <em>map</em> method. (If the parse was not successful, then calling <em>map</em> has no effect.) Normally, unless there is a system error, mapping should always complete successfully. Since mapping results in an update to the UML model, the <em>map</em> method should be called within a MagicDraw session. This can be done conveniently using the <em>AlfActionUtil.executeSession</em> method, which also ensures that any automatic Alf compilation is turned off during the session, so new compilations are not accidentally triggered by updates from the mapping process.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="122c120f-9064-4027-a2bd-0099d7c61cba"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">
       java
      </ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[AlfCompiler compiler = new AlfCompiler();
compiler.setContextElement(element);
compiler.parse(text);
if (compiler.isSuccessful()) {
	AlfActionUtil.executeSession("Map Alf", new Runnable() {
		public void run() {
			compiler.map();
			AlfActionUtil.registerDependencies(compiler);
 		}
 	});
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Rather than separately <span class="confluence-link">parsing</span> and <span class="confluence-link">mapping</span> some Alf text, you can attempt to do both with one call to the <em>compile</em> method. This method first parses some text for a given context element and, if that is successful, maps it and updates the UML model appropriately with the results of the mapping. If the parse is not successful, then compilation errors are available using the <em>getCompilerErrors</em> method, as after a call to the <em>parse</em> method. As for the <em>map</em> method, the <em>compile</em> method should be called within a MagicDraw session, since it may potentially update the UML model if a mapping is carried out.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="33649f26-e83d-42ba-8e21-89c56e23401a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">
       java
      </ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Compiler compiler = new AlfCompiler();
AlfActionUtil.executeSession("Compile Alf", new Runnable() {
	public void run() {
		compiler.compile(element, text); 
		AlfActionUtil.registerDependencies(compiler);
	}
});]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="20911721-b872-4176-bfa5-9aae19d59ee1"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">
       Related Pages
      </ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Compiler API: Parsing" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"> </p><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303989255 space=DEVG version=1 -->
## PAGE 00036: (2026x Refresh1) Compiler API: Parsing

- page_id: `303989255`
- space_key: `DEVG`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG/pages/303989255/2026x+Refresh1+Compiler+API+Parsing
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T09:44:27.571+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Developer Guides > (2026x Refresh1) Developer Guides Home > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) UML-based > (2026x Refresh1) SysML v1 > (2026x Refresh1) Alf Plugin Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Compiler API
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

*Parsing* is the process of creating an abstract syntax representation of some Alf code. If parsing is successful, the Alf compiler also performs constraint checking on the resulting abstract syntax tree. *Constraint checking* is the process of doing name resolution and validating the Alf code against the static-semantic constraints defined in the Alf specification.

A compilation takes place in the context of a specific Named Element in the UML model, called the *context element*. During parsing and constraint checking, name resolution is performed in the scope containing the context element (known, in Alf terms, as the *model scope*). After the successful mapping of Alf code to UML, the context element is updated with the results of the compilation. The context element should be an Activity, Opaque Behavior, Opaque Action or Opaque Expression

In UML, a Behavior may have a context Classifier (for example, the Class that owns a State Machine as its classifier behavior is the context Classifier for the State Machine). This use of the term "context" is unrelated to the term "context element" for an Alf compilation.

To compile some Alf code text, first set the context element using the *setContextElement* method and then call the *parse* method, passing the text. What Alf text can be validly parsed depends on what the context element is.

- If the context element is a Value Specification, then the text is assumed to be for an Alf Expression.
- If the context element is an Opaque Action, then the text may be an Alf Expression or Statement Sequence.
- Otherwise the text is assumed to be for an Alf Statement Sequence.

The Alf code associated with an Activity, Opaque Behavior, Opaque Action or Opaque Expression is known as the *Alf body* of that element. The Alf code is saved in the model differently for different kinds of Elements. However, you can use the *AlfElementUtil.getAlfBody* operation to get the Alf body of an Element (if it has one).

After the parsing process completes, you can check if it was successful by calling the *isSuccessful* method. If the parse is successful, then the resulting abstract syntax tree is rooted in an Alf Unit Definition obtained by calling the *getUnit* method, which in turn always contains an Activity Definition (which may be obtained by calling the *UnitDefinition.getDefinition* method). If there were errors, then you can retrieve them using the *get**CompilerErrors* method. A single *AlfCompiler* instance my be used to compile multiple Alf code texts, for the same or different context elements.

Alf abstract syntax classes are found in sub-packages of the package *org.modeldriven.alf.syntax,* which is from the Alf Reference Implementation, with source available [here](https://github.com/ModelDriven/Alf-Reference-Implementation/tree/master/org.modeldriven.alf/src/org/modeldriven/alf/syntax). These abstract syntax classes allow the abstract syntax tree to be navigated based on the structure of the abstract syntax metamodel defined in the Alf specification. For instance, the value of the *definition* property of a *UnitDefinition* is obtained by calling *getDefinition,* the *name* of that definition is obtained using *getName,* etc. (Note that use of the Reference Implementation outside of MagicDraw tooling is subject to its separate open-source licensing terms.)

For the purposes of triggering automatic compilation, the Alf compiler also [manages a record of the dependencies](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MAA/Dependency+Management) of Alf code in a model on other model elements. After successfully parsing some Alf code, if you want to update the dependency record based on the parse, you need to call the *AlfActionUtil.registerDependencies* method. If you do multiple parses with the same *AlfCompiler* instance, then you need to call *registerDependencies* after each parse operation.

```java

```

If there were errors, then you can retrieve them using the <em>get</em><em>CompilerErrors</em> method. A single <em>AlfCompiler</em> instance my be used to compile multiple Alf code texts, for the same or different context elements.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9ad16a2d-8894-4280-816c-b63c48c9c437"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Alf abstract syntax classes are found in sub-packages of the package <em>org.modeldriven.alf.syntax,</em> which is from the Alf Reference Implementation, with source available <a href="https://github.com/ModelDriven/Alf-Reference-Implementation/tree/master/org.modeldriven.alf/src/org/modeldriven/alf/syntax">here</a>. For instance, the value of the <em>definition</em> property of a <em>UnitDefinition</em> is obtained by calling <em>getDefinition,</em> the <em>name</em> of that definition is obtained using <em>getName,</em> etc. (Note that use of the Reference Implementation outside of MagicDraw tooling is subject to its separate open-source licensing terms.)</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">For the purposes of triggering automatic compilation, the Alf compiler also <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MAA/Dependency+Management">manages a record of the dependencies</a> of Alf code in a model on other model elements. After successfully parsing some Alf code, if you want to update the dependency record based on the parse, you need to call the <em>AlfActionUtil.registerDependencies</em> method.        java
````html
      </ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[AlfCompiler compiler = new AlfCompiler(); 
compiler.setContextElement(element); 
compiler.parse(AlfElementUtil.getAlfBody(element)); 
if (compiler.isSuccessful()) { 
	AlfActionUtil.registerDependencies(compiler); 
	UnitDefinition unit = compiler.getUnit(); 
	... 
} else { 
	for (CompilerError error: compiler.getCompilerErrors()) { 
		... 
	} 
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303989265 space=DEVG version=1 -->
## PAGE 00037: (2026x Refresh1) Compiler API: Utilities

- page_id: `303989265`
- space_key: `DEVG`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG/pages/303989265/2026x+Refresh1+Compiler+API+Utilities
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T09:44:27.985+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Developer Guides > (2026x Refresh1) Developer Guides Home > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) UML-based > (2026x Refresh1) SysML v1 > (2026x Refresh1) Alf Plugin Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Compiler API
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Instead of explicitly creating an *AlfCompiler* object and using its methods, you can alternatively parse and map Alf code using static methods provided by the *AlfActionUtil* class.

The *AlfActionUtil.parse* method takes a Named Element and, using this as the context element, parses and constraint checks the Alf body of the Element, if it has one. If the parsing and constraint checking succeed, then the method also automatically registers dependencies. The method returns a Boolean indicating whether the parse succeeded or not. If the parse fails, Compiler Errors are reported in an error message that is attached to the context element using an error Annotation (which then becomes available as an Active Validation Result, as described in [the Alf editor](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MAA/The+Alf+editor)).

```java

```

The *AlfActionUtil.compile* method takes a Named Element and, using this as the context element, both parses and maps the Alf body of the Element, if it has one. If the compilation is successful, then the method updates the context element and automatically registers dependencies. The method returns a Boolean indicating whether the parse succeeded or not. As with the *parse* method, if the compilation fails, Compiler Errors are recorded in an error message that is attached to the context element using an error Annotation. Unlike the *AlfCompiler.map* and *AlfCompiler.compile*methods, you do not need to explicitly start a MagicDraw session to call the *AlfActionUtil.compile* method, because it ensures internally that a session is started for mapping, if necessary.

```java

```

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Compiler API: Parsing' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Compiler API: Mapping' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303988973 space=DEVG version=1 -->
## PAGE 00038: (2026x Refresh1) Configure test environment

- page_id: `303988973`
- space_key: `DEVG`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG/pages/303988973/2026x+Refresh1+Configure+test+environment
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T09:44:10.795+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Developer Guides > (2026x Refresh1) Developer Guides Home > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) General > (2026x Refresh1) Plugins > (2026x Refresh1) Creating test cases
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

502787105

502787126

502787116

Test cases can be run as a regular JUnit test case using the JUnit *TestRunner* class or configuration tools such as Ant or Maven. However, some test case specific system properties should be set.

The program installation directory should be also specified using the *install.root* system property which can be passed to JVM as a runtime argument *-Dinstall.root=path_to_modeling_tool_installation_directory*.

It is possible to set up the program installation to use the floating license server or license file. For more information, see the "Providing licensing information" chapter at [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Licensing information' space=''].

Test cases may use external resources such as configuration files and projects during tests. It is recommended to keep external test resources apart from test case implementation class files. The test framework provides methods for retrieving test resources from the one specific resource directory. The resource directory for test cases can be configured by specifying the*tests.resources* system property of JVM. The tests resource property can be passed to JVM as a runtime argument

```text

```

The Log4j logger used in test cases can be configured by specifying the *test.properties* file which should be placed in *<modeling tool installation directory>\data*.

The program is started in a "silent" mode when running tests - the user interference requiring dialogs is not shown, instead of the warning "*WARN GENERAL - TRYING TO DISPLAY "<dialog title>" DIALOG IN SILENT MODE*" is logged. The stack trace is logged too if a special system property *-DprintSilentDialogStackTrace=true* is set.

502787104

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Developing plugins using IDE' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Licensing information' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303989202 space=DEVG version=1 -->
## PAGE 00039: (2026x Refresh1) Configuring cell entries - dependencies

- page_id: `303989202`
- space_key: `DEVG`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG/pages/303989202/2026x+Refresh1+Configuring+cell+entries+-+dependencies
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T09:44:25.955+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Developer Guides > (2026x Refresh1) Developer Guides Home > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) UML-based > (2026x Refresh1) Working with diagrams > (2026x Refresh1) Working with Dependency Matrix > (2026x Refresh1) Customizing a dependency matrix
- labels: ['dependency-extractor', 'configure-dependency-handlers', 'dependency-matrix-configurator', 'get-dependencies', 'dependency-entry', 'dependency-editor', 'can-create', 'create-add-actions', 'can-edit', 'create-edit-actions']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In order to get the dependencies between row and column elements in the dependency matrix, components of the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.datamodel.cell.DependencyExtractor* interface are used. These components allow to

- Define custom dependencies that can be created in a cell.
- Add shortcut menu commands for navigation (other than opening the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MT'] of the element and selecting the element in the Containment tree).
- Construct smart listener configurations that define cases when some element dependencies should be updated instantly (without the full rebuild of the matrix).

Use the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.configuration.DependencyMatrixConfigurator.configureDependencyHandlers(java.util.Collection<**DependencyExtractor>, java.util.Collection<DependencyEditor>)* method to register a custom dependency extractor. The method can remove the default dependency extractor from the collection and leave only a custom extractor.

The most important method is com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.datamodel.cell.DependencyExtractor.getDependencies(ElementNode, ElementNode). It is called to add dependencies in the matrix cell. By using custom algorithms you can create additional instances of the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.datamodel.cell.DependencyEntry* class for each cell.

###### Configuring the shortcut menu of a cell

All commands of the shortcut menu of a cell, are available only from the components of the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.datamodel.editing.DependencyEditor* interface. These components allow for

- Defining if new dependency can be created in the cell.
- Defining if current cell value can be edited.
- Adding custom actions for creating or editing dependencies.

Use the *DependencyMatrixConfigurator.configureDependencyHandlers(java.util.Collection<**DependencyExtractor>, java.util.Collection<DependencyEditor>)* method to register a custom dependency editor. The method implementation can remove the default dependency editor from the collection and leave only a custom editor.

Dependency matrix cells with the default renderer can be displayed either as editable or as read-only. If it is possible to create or delete at least one dependency in the cell, the cell is displayed as editable, that is, the cell's background is white.

When adding a new command, the *DependencyEditor.canCreate(**PersistenceManager, ElementNode, ElemenNode, AbstractMatrixCell)* method is first called and if it returns *true*, the *DependencyEditor.createAddActions(**PersistenceManager, *ElementNode*, *ElementNode*, AbstractMatrixCell, ActionsCategory, ActionsCategory, ActionsCategory)* method is called to add the command.

When editing a command, the *DependencyEditor.canEdit(**PersistenceManager, **ElementNode**, **ElementNode**, AbstractMatrixCell)* method is first called and if it returns *true*, the *DependencyEditor.createEditActions(PersistenceManager[,](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/dependencymatrix/datamodel/editing/DependencyEditor.html#createEditActions-com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.persistence.PersistenceManager-com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element-com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element-com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.datamodel.cell.AbstractMatrixCell-com.nomagic.actions.ActionsCategory-com.nomagic.actions.ActionsCategory-)**ElementNode**[,](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/dependencymatrix/datamodel/editing/DependencyEditor.html#createEditActions-com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.persistence.PersistenceManager-com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element-com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element-com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.datamodel.cell.AbstractMatrixCell-com.nomagic.actions.ActionsCategory-com.nomagic.actions.ActionsCategory-)**ElementNode**[,](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/dependencymatrix/datamodel/editing/DependencyEditor.html#createEditActions-com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.persistence.PersistenceManager-com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element-com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element-com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.datamodel.cell.AbstractMatrixCell-com.nomagic.actions.ActionsCategory-com.nomagic.actions.ActionsCategory-)AbstractMatrixCell[,](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/dependencymatrix/datamodel/editing/DependencyEditor.html#createEditActions-com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.persistence.PersistenceManager-com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element-com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element-com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.datamodel.cell.AbstractMatrixCell-com.nomagic.actions.ActionsCategory-com.nomagic.actions.ActionsCategory-)ActionsCategory[,](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/dependencymatrix/datamodel/editing/DependencyEditor.html#createEditActions-com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.persistence.PersistenceManager-com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element-com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element-com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.datamodel.cell.AbstractMatrixCell-com.nomagic.actions.ActionsCategory-com.nomagic.actions.ActionsCategory-)ActionsCategory[)](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/dependencymatrix/datamodel/editing/DependencyEditor.html#createEditActions-com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.persistence.PersistenceManager-com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element-com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element-com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.datamodel.cell.AbstractMatrixCell-com.nomagic.actions.ActionsCategory-com.nomagic.actions.ActionsCategory-)* method is called to edit the command.

## PAGE 00040: (2026x Refresh1) Configuring element Specification window

- page_id: `303989422`
- space_key: `DEVG`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG/pages/303989422/2026x+Refresh1+Configuring+element+Specification+window
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T09:44:37.576+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Developer Guides > (2026x Refresh1) Developer Guides Home > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) UML-based > (2026x Refresh1) Using and extending other UI components
- labels: ['ispecification-node-configurator', 'node', 'tree']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Open API provides a way to configure the elements' [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MT']. With your own configurator you can create new nodes or remove already existing nodes. Nodes are items of the tree visible on the left side in every [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MT'].

The *com.nomagic.magicdraw.ui.dialogs.specifications.SpecificationDialogManager*****class should be used for registering a specification dialog configurator***com.nomagic.magicdraw.ui.dialogs.specifications.configurator.ISpecificationNodeConfigurator*.

More information is available in javadoc.

****You can find the code examples in *<installation_directory>/openapi/examples/elementspecification*

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>The Open API provides a way to configure the elements' <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Specification window" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Specification windows]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. With your own configurator you can create new nodes or remove already existing nodes. Nodes are items of the tree visible on the left side in every <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>.</p><p>The <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.ui.dialogs.specifications.SpecificationDialogManager<strong> </strong></em>class should be used for registering a specification dialog configurator<em> </em><em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.ui.dialogs.specifications.configurator.ISpecificationNodeConfigurator</em>.</p><p>More information is available in javadoc.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="149c6619-33cd-40ac-aa63-ffaeb054ed5d"><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong> </strong>You can find the code examples in <em>&lt;installation_directory&gt;/openapi/examples/elementspecification</em></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303989401 space=DEVG version=1 -->
## PAGE 00041: (2026x Refresh1) Configuring node text and icon

- page_id: `303989401`
- space_key: `DEVG`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG/pages/303989401/2026x+Refresh1+Configuring+node+text+and+icon
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T09:44:35.895+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Developer Guides > (2026x Refresh1) Developer Guides Home > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) UML-based > (2026x Refresh1) Browser
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You might need to change a text or icon for some Elements(nodes) in trees. For example, the adorn standard Element icon for showing that Element is annotated like the Validation framework does.

**Option #1 for changing just a text**

The text can be changed using a custom representation provider as described in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Creating textual element representation' space='']. Using this approach, the text will be changed everywhere in UI, not just in trees.

**Option #2 for changing a node text or icon**

Use *com.nomagic.magicdraw.ui.browser.TreeNodeAdornmentManager.addIconAdornment(**IconAdornment)* to register a node icon customizer.

Use *TreeNodeAdornmentManager.addTextAdornment(TextAdornment[)](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/ui/browser/TreeNodeAdornmentManager.html#addTextAdornment-com.nomagic.magicdraw.ui.browser.TextAdornment-)* to register a node text customizer.

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Creating textual element representation' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>You might need to change a text or icon for some Elements(nodes) in trees. For example, the adorn standard Element icon for showing that Element is annotated like the Validation framework does.</p><p><strong>Option #1 for changing just a text</strong></p><p>The text can be changed using a custom representation provider as described in <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Creating textual element representation" /></ac:link>. Using this approach, the text will be changed everywhere in UI, not just in trees.</p><p><strong>Option #2 for changing a node text or icon</strong></p><p>Use <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.ui.browser.TreeNodeAdornmentManager.addIconAdornment(</em><em>IconAdornment)</em> to register a node icon customizer.</p><p>Use <em>TreeNodeAdornmentManager.addTextAdornment(TextAdornment<a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/ui/browser/TreeNodeAdornmentManager.html#addTextAdornment-com.nomagic.magicdraw.ui.browser.TextAdornment-">)</a></em> to register a node text customizer.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7f80fb8f-62ab-4a95-b916-c5df66cb9b23"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Creating textual element representation" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303989186 space=DEVG version=1 -->
## PAGE 00042: (2026x Refresh1) Configuring shortcut menus of a row or column header element and a filter panel

- page_id: `303989186`
- space_key: `DEVG`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG/pages/303989186/2026x+Refresh1+Configuring+shortcut+menus+of+a+row+or+column+header+element+and+a+filter+panel
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T09:44:25.614+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Developer Guides > (2026x Refresh1) Developer Guides Home > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) UML-based > (2026x Refresh1) Working with diagrams > (2026x Refresh1) Working with Dependency Matrix > (2026x Refresh1) Customizing a dependency matrix
- labels: ['dependency-matrix-action-registry', 'amconfigurator', 'for-row']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To add a custom command to the shortcut menu of the row or column header element, or to the one of the filter panel, use the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.configuration.DependencyMatrixActionRegistry* class.

The *com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.configuration.DependencyMatrixAMConfigurator* interface has a single method that adds custom commands. The location of a new command depends on the parameters, such as the following:

- If the collection of element nodes is empty, the command will be added to the shortcut menu of the filter panel.
- If the collection of element nodes is not empty and forRow is true , the command will be added to the shortcut menu of the row header.
- If the collection of element nodes is not empty and forRow is false the command will be added to the shortcut menu of the column header element.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>To add a custom command to the shortcut menu of the row or column header element, or to the one of the filter panel, use the <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.configuration.DependencyMatrixActionRegistry</em> class.</p><p>The <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.configuration.DependencyMatrixAMConfigurator</em> interface has a single method that adds custom commands. The location of a new command depends on the parameters, such as the following:</p><ul><li>If the collection of element nodes is empty, the command will be added to the shortcut menu of the filter panel.</li><li>If the collection of element nodes is not empty and <em>forRow</em> is <em style="line-height: 1.4285715;">true</em>, the command will be added to the shortcut menu of the row header.</li><li>If the collection of element nodes is not empty and <em>forRow</em> is <em style="line-height: 1.4285715;">false </em>the command will be added to the shortcut menu of the column header element.</li></ul></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303989176 space=DEVG version=1 -->
## PAGE 00043: (2026x Refresh1) Configuring the command bar and shortcut keys

- page_id: `303989176`
- space_key: `DEVG`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG/pages/303989176/2026x+Refresh1+Configuring+the+command+bar+and+shortcut+keys
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T09:44:25.424+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Developer Guides > (2026x Refresh1) Developer Guides Home > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) UML-based > (2026x Refresh1) Working with diagrams > (2026x Refresh1) Working with Dependency Matrix > (2026x Refresh1) Customizing a dependency matrix
- labels: ['amconfigurator', 'actions-configurator-manager', 'dependency-matrix-diagram-descriptor', 'dependency-matrix-configurator']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can add custom commands to the command bar of a dependency matrix as well as assign custom shortcut keys for the commands.

To add a new command to the command bar, add an instance of the *com.nomagic.actions.AMConfigurator* interface to the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.actions.ActionsConfiguratorsManager* class, as is shown in the following example:

```java

```

When customizing the default dependency matrix type, provide the name defined in the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.configuration.DependencyMatrixConfigurator.DEFAULT_DEPENDENCY_MATRIX_DIAGRAM_TYPE* constant*.*And when customizing a custom dependency matrix type, provide the name defined in the instance of the *DependencyMatrixConfigurator* class as well as in the **Customize Dependency Matrix Wizard**dialog.

The same code example can be used for the shortcut key configuration:

```java

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><div class="WordSection1"><p>You can add custom commands to the command bar of a dependency matrix as well as assign custom shortcut keys for the commands.</p><p>To add a new command to the command bar, add an instance of the <em>com.nomagic.actions.AMConfigurator</em> interface to the <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.actions.ActionsConfiguratorsManager</em> class, as is shown in the following example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c0b586e6-5698-4f89-bd97-44da0e21109f"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[    ActionsConfiguratorsManager.getInstance().addDiagramCommandBarConfigurator(
			DependencyMatrixConfigurator.DEFAULT_DEPENDENCY_MATRIX_DIAGRAM_TYPE, new AMConfigurator()
    {
        @Override
        public void configure(ActionsManager mngr)
        {
            mngr.addCategory(new ActionsCategory("ID", "My Category"));
        }
 
        @Override
        public int getPriority()
        {
             return AMConfigurator.HIGH_PRIORITY;
        }
    });]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></div><p>When customizing the default dependency matrix type, provide the name defined in the <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.configuration.DependencyMatrixConfigurator.DEFAULT_DEPENDENCY_MATRIX_DIAGRAM_TYPE</em> constant<em>. </em>And when customizing a custom dependency matrix type, provide the name defined in the instance of the <em>DependencyMatrixConfigurator</em> class as well as in the <strong>Customize Dependency Matrix Wizard </strong>dialog.</p><p>The same code example can be used for the shortcut key configuration:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="65e7f44d-19b8-4e4d-90f4-c1a5551bdd10"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[    ActionsConfiguratorsManager.getInstance().addDiagramShortcutsConfigurator(...)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

Built using *[ElementsFactory](https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/sysml/model/ElementsFactory.html).[createConnectionUsage](https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/sysml/model/SysMLElementsFactory.html#createConnectionUsage())()*. Create *sourceEnd* and *targetEnd* for the connection using the *[Connectors](https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/Connectors.html)* utility class. Configure the routing (chain) of the connection by passing a list of elements to*[Connectors.setEndChain()](https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/Connectors.html#setEndChain(com.dassault_systemes.modeler.kerml.model.kerml.Feature,java.util.List)).*


````html
<p class="query-text-line ng-star-inserted">Built using <em><a href="https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/sysml/model/ElementsFactory.html">ElementsFactory</a>.<a href="https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/sysml/model/SysMLElementsFactory.html#createConnectionUsage()">createConnectionUsage</a>()</em>. Create <em>sourceEnd</em> and <em>targetEnd</em> for the connection using the <em><a href="https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/Connectors.html">Connectors</a></em> utility class. Configure the routing (chain) of the connection by passing a list of elements to<em> <a href="https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/Connectors.html#setEndChain(com.dassault_systemes.modeler.kerml.model.kerml.Feature,java.util.List)">Connectors.setEndChain()</a>.</em> <br /><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0c494765-8307-4dfc-9ecc-47125ab4a425"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import com.dassault_systemes.modeler.kerml.model.Connectors;
import com.dassault_systemes.modeler.kerml.model.kerml.Feature;
import com.dassault_systemes.modeler.sysml.model.ElementsFactory;
import com.dassault_systemes.modeler.sysml.model.sysml.ConnectionUsage;
import com.dassault_systemes.modeler.sysml.model.sysml.PartUsage;
import com.dassault_systemes.modeler.sysml.model.sysml.PortUsage;
import com.nomagic.magicdraw.core.Project;

Project project = ...; 
ElementsFactory elementsFactory = ElementsFactory.get(project);
PartUsage contextPart = elementsFactory.createPartUsage();
PartUsage sourcePart = elementsFactory.createPartUsage();
PartUsage targetPart = elementsFactory.createPartUsage();
PortUsage sourcePort = elementsFactory.createPortUsage();
PortUsage targetPort = elementsFactory.createPortUsage();
ConnectionUsage connection = elementsFactory.createConnectionUsage();
connection.setOwner(contextPart);

// create ends
Feature sourceEnd = Connectors.getOrCreateEndFeature(connection, Connectors.SOURCE_INDEX);
Feature targetEnd = Connectors.getOrCreateEndFeature(connection, Connectors.TARGET_INDEX);

// define connection path (chain)
Connectors.setEndChain(sourceEnd, List.of(sourcePart, sourcePort));
Connectors.setEndChain(targetEnd, List.of(targetPart, targetPort));]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="query-text-line ng-star-inserted"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303989532 space=DEVG version=1 -->
## PAGE 00045: (2026x Refresh1) Connectors Utility Class

- page_id: `303989532`
- space_key: `DEVG`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/DEVG/pages/303989532/2026x+Refresh1+Connectors+Utility+Class
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T09:44:40.439+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Developer Guides > (2026x Refresh1) Developer Guides Home > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Developer Guide > (2026x Refresh1) SysML v2 > (2026x Refresh1) Working With Model > (2026x Refresh1) Utilities and Helpers
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In KerML and SysML v2, a `[Connector](https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/kerml/Connector.html)` represents a **relationship between features**.

Unlike simple links, connectors are defined through **feature chains** — paths that navigate through a model structure to identify what is actually being connected.

The `[com.dassault_systemes.modeler.kerml.model.Connectors](https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/Connectors.html)` utility class provides a unified API to:

- access connector ends
- navigate and manipulate feature chains
- safely create missing end features
- analyze connector structure and context

#### **Understanding Connectors in KerML**

A connector does not directly connect two elements.

Instead, it connects:

- source chain (index 0)
- target chain (index 1)

Each chain is:

- a list of [Feature](https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/kerml/Feature.html) elements
- representing a navigation path through the model
- where the last [feature](https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/kerml/Feature.html) is the actual connection point

A connector connects *paths*, not just endpoints.

#### **Getting Source and Target Ends**

Each [connector](https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/kerml/Connector.html) has two primary ends:

- source (index 0)
- target (index 1)

```java

```

These return the **end feature objects**, not the full chains.

##### Working with End Features on Types

```java

```

##### Ensuring End Features Exist

```java

```

This is especially important before assigning chains.

#### **Working with Feature Chains**

Each connector end is typically defined by a **feature chain**.

##### Get full chains

```java
sourceChain = Connectors.getSourceChain(connector);
List targetChain = Connectors.getTargetChain(connector);]]>
```

##### Get first and last elements

```java

```

- First feature → where navigation starts
- Last feature → what is actually connected

##### Working directly with an end feature

```java
chain = Connectors.getEndChain(end);
Feature last = Connectors.getEndChainLastFeature(end);]]>
```

#### **Modifying Chains**

Chains define what the connector connects. 
Changing a chain effectively rewires the connector.

##### Set source and target chains

```java

```

##### Generalized version

```java

```

##### Setting chain directly on an end

```java

```

##### Important

- Chains are applied using [reference subsetting](https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/kerml/ReferenceSubsetting.html) semantics
- You are not just assigning a list — you are modifying underlying relationships

#### **Navigating Connected Connectors**

```java
connectors = Connectors.getConnectedConnectors(feature);]]>
```

Use this for:

- dependency analysis
- graph traversal
- impact analysis

#### **Connector Identification**

##### Detect connector elements

```java

```

##### **Detect n-ary connectors**

```java

```

- Binary connector → 2 ends
- N-ary connector → 3+ ends

#### **Connector Context and Scope**

##### Check if connector belongs to a context

```java

```

##### Check if connector is package-level

```java

```

#### **Key Takeaways**

- Connectors in SysML v2 are path-based relationships , not simple links
- Each end is defined by a feature chain , not just a single element
- The [Connectors](https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/Connectors.html) utility class helps you:
  - safely access and create end features
  - navigate chains
  - modify connections correctly
  - analyze connectivity and structure

<strong>relationship between features</strong>.</p><p>Unlike simple links, connectors are defined through <strong>feature chains</strong> — paths that navigate through a model structure to identify what is actually being connected.</p><p>The <code><a href="https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/Connectors.html">com.dassault_systemes.modeler.kerml.model.Connectors</a></code> utility class provides a unified API to:</p><ul><li data-uuid="e8a779e2-8c77-41a1-86e9-d496ce222078">access connector ends</li><li data-uuid="db20eedd-3c30-4cdc-9b93-78187c619caf">navigate and manipulate feature data-uuid="0021b65b-8d78-4b3c-a1bd-2642f068c900"><strong>source chain (index 0)</strong></li><li data-uuid="e15e1959-c87d-4df2-982e-52b8a64be641"><strong>target chain (index 1)</strong></li></ul><p>Each chain is:</p><ul><li data-uuid="378607de-dc0f-45ab-9d7a-12fd0d23f8cd">a list of <code><a data-uuid="8ef746f2-63ab-4f3d-9c89-84412b2ddfdd">representing a navigation path through the model</li><li data-uuid="a82f32ec-bc96-4cb7-a15c-639a2bee6bc6">where the <strong>last <a href="https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/kerml/Feature.html">feature</a> is the actual connection point</strong></li></ul><blockquote><p>A connector connects <em>paths</em>, not just endpoints.</p></blockquote><h2><strong>Getting Source and Target Ends</strong></h2><p>Each <a data-uuid="7950d766-3fab-4eb1-a4db-8a38d5926ed3"><strong>source (index 0)</strong></li><li data-uuid="7e5647cb-48a6-44eb-bcc7-ad4cc518cc3d"><strong>target (index 1)</strong> </li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="96666544-c579-448c-ade5-af8908ae1540"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import com.dassault_systemes.modeler.kerml.model.Connectors;
````html
import com.dassault_systemes.modeler.kerml.model.kerml.Connector;
import com.dassault_systemes.modeler.kerml.model.kerml.Feature;

Connector connector = ...;

Feature sourceEnd = Connectors.getSourceEnd(connector);
Feature targetEnd = Connectors.getTargetEnd(connector);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br />These return the <strong>end feature objects</strong>, not the full chains.</p><h3>Working with End Features on Types </h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="df966362-d081-4ae1-873b-bb5e781ad278"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import com.dassault_systemes.modeler.kerml.model.kerml.Type;

Type type = ...;

Feature source = Connectors.getEndFeature(type, Connectors.SOURCE_INDEX);
Feature target = Connectors.getEndFeature(type, Connectors.TARGET_INDEX);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Ensuring End Features Exist</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ce3e7be3-6a29-4586-b5b5-dfbfb8fa316e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Feature ensuredSource = Connectors.getOrCreateEndFeature(connector, Connectors.SOURCE_INDEX);
Feature ensuredTarget = Connectors.getOrCreateEndFeature(connector, Connectors.TARGET_INDEX);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p> This is especially important before assigning chains.</p><h2><strong>Working with Feature Chains</strong></h2><p>Each connector end is typically defined by a <strong>feature chain</strong>.</p><h3>Get full chains</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4848a6ec-115b-4076-bd53-6a4914992503"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import java.util.List;

List<Feature> sourceChain = Connectors.getSourceChain(connector);
List<Feature> targetChain = Connectors.getTargetChain(connector);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><br />Get first and last elements</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f3a1e3d0-2564-47ee-b2da-3220cbdee956"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Feature sourceFirst = Connectors.getEndChainFirstFeature(connector, Connectors.SOURCE_INDEX);
Feature sourceLast = Connectors.getEndChainLastFeature(connector, Connectors.SOURCE_INDEX);

Feature targetFirst = Connectors.getEndChainFirstFeature(connector, Connectors.TARGET_INDEX);
Feature targetLast = Connectors.getEndChainLastFeature(connector, Connectors.TARGET_INDEX);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><ul><li data-uuid="32199d5b-0437-43e2-8d67-62775f53a417"><strong>First feature</strong> → where navigation starts</li><li data-uuid="4883dcbf-d385-47ab-8be7-d15645bf13ea"><strong>Last feature</strong> → what is actually connected</li></ul><h3>Working directly with an end feature</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c481eea0-fe6e-4f88-806f-ee99af462b32"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Feature end = Connectors.getSourceEnd(connector);

List<Feature> chain = Connectors.getEndChain(end);
Feature last = Connectors.getEndChainLastFeature(end);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h2><strong>Modifying Chains</strong></h2><p>Chains define what the connector connects.<br />Changing a chain effectively rewires the connector.</p><h3>Set source and target chains </h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4884c7e0-fe51-4326-9c45-fe12898b1413"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import java.util.List;

boolean sourceUpdated = Connectors.setSourceChain(connector, newSourceChain);
boolean targetUpdated = Connectors.setTargetChain(connector, newTargetChain);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><br />Generalized version</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9173b481-b746-4d88-9eab-2b86b3c27602"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[boolean updated = Connectors.setEndChain(connector, Connectors.SOURCE_INDEX, newSourceChain);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><br />Setting chain directly on an end</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d08fc565-605a-41c5-b5db-f03808b07043"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Feature end = Connectors.getOrCreateEndFeature(connector, Connectors.SOURCE_INDEX);

boolean updated = Connectors.setEndChain(end, newSourceChain);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><br />Important</h3><ul><li data-uuid="89002e88-daf9-4765-87fb-3374cc7e16c4">Chains are applied using <strong><a href="https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/kerml/ReferenceSubsetting.html">reference subsetting</a> semantics</strong></li><li data-uuid="c6ac0f4e-c13b-4dfb-89a8-11cf13cc8be7">You are not just assigning a list — you are modifying underlying relationships</li></ul><h2><strong>Navigating Connected Connectors</strong></h2><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e8e6ac38-157d-4a81-ae2e-a106a1b3a03d"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import com.dassault_systemes.modeler.kerml.model.kerml.Feature;
import java.util.Collection;

Feature feature = ...;

Collection<Connector> connectors = Connectors.getConnectedConnectors(feature);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br />Use this for:</p><ul><li data-uuid="3b85dfb6-9965-4f2b-8aa6-d45bf1ffba6a">dependency analysis</li><li data-uuid="279bb373-613a-49d1-84fd-eeb02a6efe53">graph traversal</li><li data-uuid="09c03b21-e74e-42aa-ad65-6ff4da12a1a8">impact analysis</li></ul><h2><strong>Connector Identification</strong></h2><h3>Detect connector elements</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="277a80dd-e15b-4ae6-b2bb-4752c6397c0c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import com.dassault_systemes.modeler.kerml.model.kerml.Element;

Element element = ...;

boolean isConnector = Connectors.isConnectorOrRepresentsConnector(element);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><strong>Detect n-ary connectors</strong></h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="438638af-f7b0-4fe8-8b19-54d7c6fa81d6"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import com.dassault_systemes.modeler.foundation.model.ModelElement;

ModelElement modelElement = ...;

boolean isNary = Connectors.isNaryConnector(modelElement);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><ul><li data-uuid="0429467a-6317-479f-93a2-17429c5a3a3a"><strong>Binary connector</strong> → 2 ends</li><li data-uuid="31c29d05-3f5b-4c6f-a88e-453d79a0356b"><strong>N-ary connector</strong> → 3+ ends</li></ul><h2><strong>Connector Context and Scope</strong></h2><h3>Check if connector belongs to a context</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4ca67bcd-e4af-4036-80fb-0d8cc6f69525"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import com.dassault_systemes.modeler.kerml.model.kerml.Type;

Type context = ...;

boolean isContext = Connectors.isContext(context, connector);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><br />Check if connector is package-level</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2629e910-ccc8-4c28-9abc-32acc5422479"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[boolean isPackageLevel = Connectors.isPackageLevel(connector);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h2><strong>Key Takeaways</strong></h2><ul><li data-uuid="08699215-4ab0-4c56-ab96-ba78187dc580">Connectors in SysML v2 are <strong>path-based relationships</strong>, not simple links</li><li data-uuid="1dee4cfd-4799-4467-8cf6-7fa1f7d422a8">Each end is defined by a <strong>feature chain</strong>, not just a single element</li><li data-uuid="df7a419a-df62-46b8-9d18-5de905a5ae23">The <code><a href="https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2026xRefresh1/com/dassault_systemes/modeler/kerml/model/Connectors.html">Connectors</a></code> utility class helps you:<ul><li>safely access and create end features</li><li>navigate chains</li><li>modify connections correctly</li><li>analyze connectivity and structure</li></ul></li></ul>
````
